banner.headline: {count, plural, one {# active incident} other {# active incidents}}

incidents.title: Article 14 Incident Reporting
incidents.subtitle: Track and report security incidents and actively-exploited vulnerabilities to ENISA within the CRA reporting windows.

empty.title: No incidents yet
empty.description: When a security incident or actively-exploited vulnerability is detected, file it here to start the 24h / 72h / 14d reporting clock.
empty.noProductDescription: Incidents are reported against a specific product. Add your first product to start tracking incidents and the CRA 24h / 72h / 14d reporting clock.

new.title: Report a new incident
new.description: Filing an incident starts the Article 14 reporting clock. You have 24h for the early warning, 72h for the incident report, and 14 days for the final report.
new.descriptionPlaceholder: What happened, when you became aware, and who is investigating.

title.title: Incident title
title.body: Short, recognisable name for the incident — something a teammate can identify at a glance in a list (e.g. "CVE-2024-XXXX exploited in firmware v2.3", "Customer-reported privilege escalation"). Used in the PDF filing to ENISA.
title.ref: CRA Article 14(1) — the early warning filed within 24 hours must at minimum name the incident; a clear title is the first field ENISA's intake form asks for.

type.title: Incident type
type.body: Pick "Security incident" for a compromise that has already affected a product or its users (data leak, ransomware, account takeover). Pick "Exploited vulnerability" when you have evidence that a known CVE in your product is being actively exploited in the wild.
type.ref: CRA Article 14(1) — both categories trigger the reporting clock, but the intake form ENISA uses distinguishes between them and routes them to different downstream teams.

severity.title: Severity
severity.body: Subjective assessment of user impact. Critical = widespread harm or imminent safety risk. High = significant impact on affected users. Medium = limited impact, workaround exists. Low = minimal impact. Auditors expect your assessment to match the evidence.
severity.ref: CRA Article 14(2)(a) — the severity classification is part of every phase's report. It drives whether and how you must also notify affected users under Article 14(6).

affectedProducts.title: Affected products
affectedProducts.body: Select every product in your catalogue that is or might be affected. The list is used to generate the "Affected products" section of the 24h / 72h / 14d reports. Selecting more than strictly necessary is better than missing one.
affectedProducts.ref: CRA Article 14(2)(b) — each phase's report must identify the affected products. Incomplete product lists are the most common reason reports get bounced back by ENISA.

description.title: Description
description.body: Plain-English summary of what happened, when you became aware, who is investigating, and what you know so far. Copy-pasted verbatim into the early-warning PDF, so skip confidential detail and stick to facts that you can publish.
description.ref: CRA Article 14(1)(a) — the 24-hour early warning must at minimum include a general description of the incident; you can refine it in the 72-hour report once you know more.

detail.confirmClose: Close this incident? The timeline will stop, but the record is retained.

phaseA.title: 24-hour early warning
phaseA.description: First notice to ENISA and your CSIRT. Confirm awareness, initial impact, and whether you suspect malicious activity.
phaseA.placeholder: Describe the initial indicators, scope of awareness, and any immediate containment actions.

phaseB.title: 72-hour incident report
phaseB.description: Full notification with impact assessment, root cause so far, and compromised data categories.
phaseB.placeholder: Root cause, impact, affected components and users, remediation in progress.

phaseC.title: 14-day final report
phaseC.description: Final report with the incident's cause, severity, remediation, and mitigation measures.
phaseC.placeholder: Final cause analysis, remediation taken, residual risk, lessons learned.

userNotify.title: Notify affected users
userNotify.description: Article 14 requires manufacturers to inform affected users without undue delay and provide mitigation guidance. Captured verbatim into the audit trail.
userNotify.placeholder: What happened, what users should do, and where to get updates.

srp.title: ENISA Single Reporting Platform
srp.description: Package each stage for the EU Single Reporting Platform: download a structured submission, record the reference number the SRP returns, and copy the key fields.
